---
title: "Manuscript Title"
authors:
  - name: Person1
    affiliations:
      - ref: ufl
    corresponding: true
    email: person1@email.edu
  - name: Second Author
    affiliations:
      - ref: ufl
      - ref: aff2
  - name: Third Author
    affiliations:
      - ref: aff2
affiliations:
  - id: ufl
    name: University of Florida
    department: Department of Name
    city: Gainesville
    state: FL
  - id: aff2
    name: Second Institution
    department: Department of Name
    city: City
    state: ST
date: today
date-format: "YYYY-MM-DD"
format:
  docx:
    # reference-doc: custom-reference.docx   # optional: use a Word template for journal formatting
    toc: false
    number-sections: false
bibliography: references.bib
csl: ecology.csl    # download from https://www.zotero.org/styles
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---
```{r setup}
# load any required packages here
# although shouldn't be many since all analysis and figures should be done already
```

# Abstract

*250-300 words. Background, methods, key results, conclusions.*

**Keywords:** keyword1, keyword2, keyword3

---

# Introduction

*Background and motivation. What is the problem and why does it matter? What gap does this study fill?*

*End with a clear statement of your objectives or research questions.*

# Introduction

Understanding ecological dynamics requires careful attention to how complexity
emerges from simple rules [@May1976]. This analysis was conducted using a
reproducible workflow built on the simulation study project template
[@Rudolph2026simtemplate]. You can find more information on citations
[here](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/citations.html).

---

# Methods

## Simulation Design

*Describe your simulation framework. What parameters did you vary? What was held constant? How many replicates?*

*Reference your simulation scripts:* `R/simulations/`
```{r simulation-params}
# Load and display simulation parameter table if useful
# params <- read_csv(here("outputs", "simulation_results", "param_grid.csv"))
```

## Statistical Analysis

*How did you summarize and analyze simulation outputs?*

---

# Results

*Present results in a logical order matching your methods. Reference figures and tables by label.*
```{r load-results}
# Load summarized simulation outputs
# results <- read_csv(here("outputs", "simulation_results", "summary.csv"))
```

## Section 1

*Text referencing* @fig-example *and inline results like* `r 2 + 2`.
```{r fig-example}
#| label: fig-example
#| fig-cap: "Caption describing what this figure shows."
#| fig-width: 6.5
#| fig-height: 4

# Either build figure inline or load from figures/
# knitr::include_graphics(here("figures", "fig01_example.png"))
```

## Section 2

---

# Discussion

*Interpret your results in the context of the literature. What do your simulations tell us that empirical data alone cannot?*

## Main finding 1

## Main finding 2

## Limitations

*Be honest about assumptions baked into your simulations and what they mean for generalizability.*

---

# Conclusions



---

# Acknowledgements

*Funding, data providers, HPC resources (e.g., UF Research Computing), field or lab support, this template*

---

# Author Contributions

*CRediT taxonomy recommended: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Validation, Formal Analysis, Investigation, Data Curation, Writing – Original Draft, Writing – Review & Editing, Visualization, Supervision, Funding Acquisition.*

---

# Data Availability

*Where can data be accessed? If restricted, explain why and how to request access.*

---

# References

::: {#refs}
:::
